summary: DHH describes the process of re-syncing Basecamp with the latest version of Rails after months of working on separate branches. During the re-sync, he was able to refactor significant cruft out of the codebase, resulting in a 10% reduction in code size even while adding new features. He reflects on the satisfaction of seeing a production system shrink in lines of code as functionality grows, calling it a sign of well-maintained maintainability. He notes Basecamp's control-flow and model logic sits just above 4 KLOC and aims to push it lower.
key_points: Uncoupling Basecamp from Rails trunk during rapid framework development was sensible but created dangerous mental overhead from API version mismatches
Re-syncing Basecamp with current Rails allowed refactoring that removed 10% of the codebase while adding new features
Shrinking code while growing functionality is the hallmark of well-maintained software, and DHH felt it concretely for the first time in a production system
Basecamp's core logic at ~4 KLOC demonstrates the power of a high-level framework to keep a real product small and manageable
money_quotes: It's about features because customers rarely care about what you had to do to get there.
The code base lost about 10% of it's weight while I was adding a bunch of new features.
So were going higher in terms of functionality and going down in terms of KLOC.

That's what's supposed to happen when you maintain your maintainability, but it's probably one of the first times I've really felt that to be true for a system that had been in production for almost six months.
With the control-flow and model logic hovering at just above 4 KLOC, it's going to feel even greater as I'm able to simplify further.
key_insight: A maturing framework should let you add features while reducing code size — growing functionality and shrinking complexity at the same time is the true measure of maintainability.
